patterns.name: Missing Context Clues
patterns.what: Sometimes picks a word that makes sense on its own but misses the specific hint hidden in the rest of the sentence.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Shadow wrote 'While' instead of 'Besides', missing the clue word 'also' later in the sentence which shows information is being added.
Shadow wrote 'held' instead of 'placed', missing the logical clue that the guide has to put the model into the blind person's hands so they can feel it.
Shadow chose 'released' instead of 'deported', missing the clue that the pirates were being sent back to their 'home country'.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a blank, look for the **hidden clues** in the same sentence or the sentence before it.
- Highlight connector words like **'also'**, **'therefore'**, or **'so that'**—they tell you if the sentence is adding information, showing a result, or giving a reason.
- Ask yourself: 'Does my word fit perfectly with the clues around it?'

patterns.name: Common Phrase Mix-Up
patterns.what: Occasionally fills in blanks with words that mean the right thing but don't quite fit the standard English expression.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Shadow wrote 'no problem as challenging' instead of the standard phrase 'no longer as challenging'.
Shadow wrote 'first it takes' instead of the common expression 'all it takes'.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see a short phrase with a blank, try reading it aloud in your head to see if it sounds like a natural English expression.
- If you are stuck, try substituting common small words like **'all'**, **'longer'**, or **'more'**.
- Read the whole phrase together (e.g., **'all it takes'**) to check its rhythm.

patterns.name: Reading Poster Details
patterns.what: Tends to overlook small but important details in visual texts, such as absolute words or specific conditions.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Shadow chose an option saying registration is needed for 'all' activities, missing that the poster likely had exceptions.
Shadow chose an option about using 'electronic devices' for survival skills, which wasn't supported by the poster.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When answering questions about posters or flyers, treat absolute words as warning signs.
- Circle words like **'all'**, **'must'**, **'only'**, or **'every'** in the options.
- Always double-check the poster to see if the rule truly applies to **everyone** or just some people.

patterns.name: Tricky Word Spellings
patterns.what: Sometimes trips up on spelling words that have double letters or tricky vowel sounds.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Shadow added an extra 'r' when spelling 'apparently'.
Shadow mixed up the vowel team, spelling 'squeal' as 'squeel'.
Shadow spelled 'virtue' as 'vurtue', mixing up the 'ir' and 'ur' sounds.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When editing spelling mistakes, break the word down into its sounds and syllables.
- Watch out for **double letters** (like the 'p' in apparently).
- Pay attention to **vowel teams** (like 'ea' in squeal or 'ir' in virtue). If it looks a bit strange, try writing it out a few different ways on rough paper to see which one looks right.
